# Unofficial Meeting Notes
## Conroe Independent School District Board of Trustees

## Meeting Called to Order

## Board Workshop: Construction Update (Information Only — No Action)

Mr. Foster provided a comprehensive construction update covering all active 2023 bond projects and remaining 2019 bond work.

### Key Highlights:

**Overall Bond Status (2023 Bond):**
- 37 proposed contracts total
- 8 contracts substantially complete (2 new campuses opened August 2025; 17 existing locations completed)
- 20 contracts in construction (6 new campuses, 29 existing locations)
- 4 contracts in design; 5 on deck for future design

**Conroe High School (Final 2019 Bond Project):**
- 12-year master plan project; total cost approximately **$232 million**
- Original board chose to preserve the legacy/historic building rather than build new
- 27-foot elevation change within the building footprint; 33 retaining walls
- **Substantial completion: August 3, 2026** for the 2026–27 school year
- New front entry, auditorium, and black box theater under construction
- Moving to 7-day work week; cranes arriving in ~2 weeks for final steel erection

**Fowler Intermediate (Opening August 2026):**
- Building progressing well; road/utility permitting from FM 1314 authorized that day
- Sewer connection work underway with developer commitment for mid-July readiness
- Road is a private driveway (district expense); city requested a gate

**Casey Arnold Elementary (Opening August 2026):**
- Neighborhood site with utilities already connected
- Substantial completion expected May 2026
- In good shape for the timeline

**Grand Oaks 9th Grade/Junior High Building:**
- 9th grade side opens January 2027; junior high side opens August 2027
- Same physical building with separate entrances

**Timber Mill High School (Opening August 2027):**
- On schedule; masonry and track work progressing
- Current construction contract: just under **$308 million**

**Cartwright Junior High (Opening August 2027):**
- Shares site with Fowler; near 100% dried-in
- On schedule

**Caney Creek 9th Grade (Opening August 2027):**
- Approximately $3 million over original bond budget (due to site infrastructure costs)
- Private utility provider engaged for water/wastewater
- General contractor: Duratech (also on Cartwright)

**2027 Opening Summary:**
- Four major buildings opening: Timber Mill HS, Cartwright JH, Caney Creek 9th Grade, Grand Oaks JH

**Renovations & Gym Additions Completed:**
- Buckalew, Hailey, Ford, and Creighton gym additions — opened January 2026 (substantially complete; punch list work ongoing)
- Hailey and Ford also received capacity additions (~100 seats each)

**The Woodlands High School Renovation:**
- Auditorium scaffolding removed; new acoustical treatments visible; seats being reinstalled
- Goal: auditorium usable during spring semester
- Ground floor work continues through December 2026

**Oak Ridge High School:**
- 9th grade campus major renovation ongoing; completion expected summer 2026
- Phase 2 (fine arts) building pad under construction; structural steel in Houston ready to ship; completion mid-year December 2026
- Phase 3 (athletics) identified for potential future bond referendum
- Trustee May raised concern about aged furniture not being replaced during renovation; Mr. Foster noted renovation budgets historically did not include furniture but committed to working with Ms. Garza to assess needs

**Lighting Upgrades:**
- Most campuses have been or will be upgraded to LED by end of bond program
- Galatas Elementary is the one exception requiring additional work
- Trustee Odenweller emphasized lighting impacts learning, safety, and atmosphere

**Knox Junior High:**
- Major renovation including new admin section and library (swapping positions within building)
- On schedule for August 2026 completion

**McCullough Junior High:**
- Gym addition (3rd gym), classroom additions, library relocation
- On schedule for August 2026
- Trustees Odenweller and Horton noted concerns about inconsistent finishes (flooring, wall textures) in renovated hallways; Mr. Foster indicated the team is exploring options within budget

**Washington High School:**
- New building being constructed on same property; move-in targeted December 2027
- Will also house the district's virtual school
- Board and Dr. Vinson discussed vision for revitalizing the campus and community, potential ESA implications, and separating the alternative discipline campus
- Mr. Foster emphasized need for a plan to decommission the existing building (expensive to operate)

**Agricultural Facilities:**
- Three sites under construction (Bootie Road, Conroe/fairgrounds site, Robinson Road)
- Bootie Road awaiting water well permit (on Lone Star Groundwater Conservation District's March 10 agenda)

**Caney Creek Transportation Center:**
- Expansion from 2019 bond; contractor looking to turn over early summer

**Critical Infrastructure Projects:**
- Multiple campuses receiving roofing, HVAC, safety/security upgrades
- Safety/security upgrades include: interior secure doorways, exterior cameras, 100% card reader access on exterior doors (moving toward keyless operation)
- All campuses will have standardized safety/security upgrades by grade level upon completion

**Remaining Projects:**
- Final projects include admin building (Sadler), Hawk, testing/assessment, Woodlands Transportation, police command center, Caney Creek wastewater treatment plant
- Student facilities prioritized before faculty/admin facilities

### SB11 (School Prayer Policy) — Multiple Speakers Opposed:
- **Danell Cass:** Opposed SB11; distinguished existing silent prayer rights from organized religious reading; cited low student performance in core subjects

- **Laurel Stevens:** Christian opposing the policy on pluralism grounds; urged preserving religious freedom
- **Destiny Milton:** Criticized lack of transparency by labeling the item with a vague statute number; raised logistical concerns and litigation costs; noted Paxton's promise to cover legal costs has not materialized (district spent ~$100,000 in legal fees since October)

### Other Topics:

- **B. Gasa:** Updated on International Mothers' Committee progress (7 of 20 topics completed; 90% attendance)

- **Mark Fusa:** Described ongoing harassment of his autistic son at Grangerland Intermediate over noise-cancelling headphones in his IEP; challenged $7,000+ public records cost estimate; urged accountability
- **Saresa Kenny:** Opposed challenges to *Angry Spirit* and *Ann of West Philly*; noted neither contains explicit content; questioned the pattern of challenges

### 7E: CPAC — Special Education Parent Advisory Council
- Kendra Wiggins presented the proposal for a board-recognized advisory body
- District special education statistics: 11,163 special ed students (16%); 6,175 504 students (7.8%); combined 23.8% of district
- Noted significant funding losses in special education
- Structure: Advisory capacity only; board-appointed parent members; quarterly meetings; annual board reporting
- Timeline: Board approval → planning/recruiting → finalized plan by June 1 → official launch August 31
- Multiple trustees expressed strong support; discussed selection process, policy development, and learning from failures in other districts
- Trustee Gambino read prepared statement emphasizing partnership, trust-building, and structured collaboration
- **Vote: Approved 6-0-1** (one abstention)

## Teaching & Learning: Math Curriculum Adoption (K–12)

### Background:
- Current math materials do not meet HQIM (Highly Qualified Instructional Materials) criteria
- 7th grade math identified as a district focus area
- Guiding coalition of 127 members (112 classroom teachers) conducted review
- Community feedback: 71 responses K–6; 34 responses 7–12 (board members noted this was not a statistically representative sample)

### Process:
- Materials reviewed December 9 through January 27
- Educator surveys, publisher presentations, community review periods, virtual showcases
- TEA "Science of Math" principles shared with coalition (instructional hierarchy, direct explicit instruction, automaticity with math facts, spaced/interleaved practice, conceptual + procedural learning)

### K–6 Recommendations from Guiding Coalition:
1. **Savvas Learning Company — Envision Plus Texas Math** (1st recommendation)
2. **Accelerate Learning — STEMscopes Texas Math** (2nd recommendation)

**Key discussion points:**
- Envision Plus Texas is a brand-new product (not the same as current Envision Math 2.0)
- Received perfect IMRA scores in all grade levels and rubric areas
- Features: AI planning tools, MAP Growth integration, Canvas/Eduporia integration, robust emergent bilingual supports, Brainingcamp Live digital manipulatives, parent portal, blended print + digital options
- STEMscopes: Also perfect IMRA scores; 5E model; Texas-based company; QR code video tutorials; but limited language support beyond English/Spanish, no direct MAP integration, heavier lesson prep
- iReady Texas was highest-rated in educator survey but not recommended by coalition due to requiring navigation of two separate curricula, lower IMRA scores, no MAP integration

### 7–12 Recommendations:
- Savvas recommended as 1st or 2nd choice for all courses **except 7th grade** (where STEMscopes was 1st and McGraw Hill was 2nd)
- Coalition members cited concern that Savvas appeared too similar to current materials (though many were unaware it was a completely new product)
- Advanced courses: Separate publisher recommendations for AP Statistics, AP Calculus AB/BC, and Multivariable Calculus

### Financial Considerations:
- Savvas offers **15% discount** for vertical K–Algebra 2 adoption (over $1 million savings on 4-year contract)
- 4-year contract recommended over 2-year for cost savings
- Curriculum auditors confirmed recommendation would be to implement HQIM materials

### Votes:

**Savvas K through Algebra 2 (4-year contract):**
- **Vote: Approved 6-1** (Trustee Dungan opposed, citing desire to wait for additional data)

**McGraw Hill for Pre-Calculus + Cosenza & Associates for Algebraic Reasoning (4-year contract):**
- **Vote: Approved 7-0**

**AP/Advanced Courses — DFW (AP Statistics), Pearson (AP Calculus AB & BC), Savvas (Multivariable Calculus) (4-year contract):**
- **Vote: Approved 7-0**

## Board Policy — First Reading (Discussion Only)

### GKD Local — Facility Rentals
Mr. McCord presented a comprehensive overview of the current facility rental program and proposed changes.

**Current Situation:**
- District currently operates at a **net loss of ~$148,000** on outside facility rentals
- Additional **$72,000 loss** from expanded free campus use by PTOs/booster clubs (changed from 2-free-events limit in 2023)
- Electricity costs are the primary driver of losses
- District rental rates are comparable to surrounding districts

**Proposed Changes Discussed:**
- Clearly defined terms in policy
- Insurance requirement for all renters (except PTAs/PTOs/booster clubs), naming CISD
- Extended rental window (currently 3 months out)
- Reduced rental period for after-school extracurricular businesses (2 hours → 1 hour)
- Allowing teachers to conduct for-profit tutoring after contract hours
- One unified rental website (currently separate portals)

**Financial Concerns:**
- Reducing after-school rental from 2 hours to 1 hour = ~$100 net loss per event
- Potential 50 new after-school setups could mean ~$180,000 additional annual loss
- Possible need for additional FTE ($65,000 burdened cost)
- Teacher tutoring at market rates ($75–85/hr) could draw away district-funded tutors ($30/hr)
- Surrounding districts split on allowing teacher tutoring on campus (Klein, Spring = yes; Tomball, Humble, Katy = no)

**Board Discussion:**
- Trustee Dawson: "I'm not okay using taxpayer funds to subsidize for-profit companies."
- Trustee Semmler explained original intent was to lower costs for families and provide after-school access, especially for less-privileged students
- Trustee Horton raised concerns about total potential losses ($300K–$400K) and where funds would come from
- Board requested workshop with different scenarios showing cost recovery options
- President Odenweller emphasized need for a policy the district can actually comply with and that doesn't lose money

**No action taken — first reading only. Workshop with financial scenarios requested.**

## Open Session Action Items (Post-Executive Session)

### Library Material Challenges

**Motion:** Trustee Dawson moved to **not accept** the book challenges on both *Ann of West Philly* (by Ivy Noelle Weir) and *Angry Spirit* (by Anita Cray), keeping them in their current library locations.

**Discussion:**
- President Odenweller expressed concerns about age-appropriateness of both books at PreK–4 campuses
  - *Ann of West Philly*: Noted relationship themes; suggested 5th grade and above would be more appropriate (TEKS relationship instruction begins in 5th grade)
  - *Angry Spirit*: Described ghost illustrations as "extremely graphic" for young children; confirmed by Dr. Winkler's assessment
- Trustee Semmler argued *Ann of West Philly* is the same story as *Anne of Green Gables* in graphic novel form
- Trustee Dungan raised broader concerns about library cataloging systems and age-appropriate book placement
- Dr. Vinson acknowledged the concern and committed to finding solutions for categorization
- Discussion about whether library software (Follett) could restrict checkouts by grade level — currently not implemented at that granularity
- Motion to call the question approved

**Vote on not accepting the challenges (books remain): Approved 5-2**

### SB11 — School Prayer and Bible Reading Policy

**Motion:** Trustee Dawson moved that those **not in favor** of adopting the resolution show by raised hand (effectively a motion to reject the resolution).

**Discussion:**
- Trustee Dawson cited two points:
  1. State-mandated instructional minutes (435 minutes for 3rd grade) leave no time for a dedicated prayer period
  2. Read directly from SB11 noting it explicitly states students and employees can already pray and read religious texts during non-designated periods — "We are free to pray now. This bill does not change that."
- Trustee Semmler concurred: "I was shocked to read this bill in detail and see that it did nothing more than really reiterate what's already in existence."
- President Odenweller: Students already have the ability to pray; parents accept the charge of faith in the home

**Vote to NOT adopt SB11 resolution: Approved 7-0**

### Montgomery Trace 4 — Annexation/Detachment Hearing
- **Motion:** Move hearing from March 3 to March 2
- **Vote: Approved 7-0**

### Board Job Duties — Reimbursement Standards
- Trustee Gambino motioned to set clear standards in board policy for reimbursement of advocacy and training expenses for trustees

**Significant exchange occurred:**
- President Odenweller raised concerns about Trustee Semmler's taxpayer-funded trip to TASB's Federal Advocacy Conference in DC without informing the board president or having board-approved legislative priorities
- Trustee Semmler responded that she received superintendent authorization, attended educational sessions, transmitted notes to the district, and discussed removing DEI-based performance standards with legislators
- Trustee Semmler then cited instances where other board members (including President Odenweller) acted without full board authorization: signing in as CISD board president at a Senate hearing, directing purchase of Ten Commandments posters, and accommodating CPAC with district resources
- President Odenweller disputed characterizations and noted she was representing the district when attending the cheerleading event
- Motion to end discussion approved

**Vote to develop clear reimbursement/advocacy expense standards in board policy: Approved 5-2**

## ⚑ Items to Watch

- **CPAC Authorization (6-0-1):** One trustee abstained on creating the Special Education Parent Advisory Council. Parents should monitor the planning timeline (finalized plan by June 1, launch August 31) and the member selection process.
- **Math Curriculum: Savvas K–Algebra 2 (6-1):** Trustee Dungan voted against, citing desire for additional data before committing to a 4-year contract. This is a significant district-wide instructional materials adoption affecting all K–Algebra 2 students.
- **Library Challenge Vote (5-2):** *Ann of West Philly* and *Angry Spirit* remain in libraries, but the split vote and discussion about age-appropriate placement at PreK–4 campuses signal this issue may resurface. Dr. Vinson committed to exploring library cataloging solutions.
- **Motion to Move Library Discussion to Open Session (4-3):** The narrow vote to waive attorney-client privilege and discuss library challenges publicly reflects ongoing board tension about transparency in executive session.
- **Board Reimbursement Policy Standards (5-2):** Contentious discussion about trustee travel and advocacy expenses revealed significant disagreements about board authority and individual trustee actions. New policy standards are being developed.
- **GKD Facility Rental Policy (First Reading — Deferred):** Current facility rentals operate at a ~$148,000 net loss; proposed changes could increase losses to $300K–$400K. Workshop with financial scenarios requested before second reading. Impacts after-school programs, tutoring access, and PTO/booster club events.
- **Caney Creek 9th Grade Campus:** Approximately $3 million over original bond budget due to site infrastructure costs — worth monitoring for budget implications.
- **Oak Ridge HS Furniture Concern:** Trustee May flagged that aged furniture is not being replaced during renovation; administration committed to assessing needs with Ms. Garza.
- **Washington High School Redevelopment:** Board discussion touched on ESA implications, separating the alternative discipline campus, and decommissioning the existing building — significant long-term planning decisions ahead.
- **SB11 Prayer Resolution — Unanimously Rejected (7-0):** Despite significant public comment, the board unanimously declined to adopt the resolution, noting existing prayer rights are already protected. District has spent ~$100,000 in related legal fees since October.
- **Public Comment Concerns:** Multiple speakers raised transparency issues including vague agenda labeling, library challenges discussed in closed session, and a $7,000+ public records cost estimate. A member of the audience also objected that public comment sign-up paperwork was removed during the meeting.
